checks.evidence: adaptive accuracy 85% < 100%: lossy compression (recall 90%) + relevance error (5%) are modeled, not assumed away
needles clustered past the dumb zone: adaptive 85% vs naive 46%
rung-3 real mimo needle-in-haystack: recall 100% (6/6) over 30977 tokens — validates retrieval is tractable, not the adaptive advantage

limitations: Deterministic information-retention benchmark of the context layer, not a live-LLM comprehension run.
The ~40% naive reliable fraction is the documented Fable-5/LLM dumb-zone, a stated baseline rather than re-measured.

Compression loss (recall) and relevance-misclassification rates are modeled parameters; the headline is expected accuracy at the 70% target with a seed band, not a single deterministic ceiling (which the red-team showed is a threshold knife-edge).

red_team_caveats: Single model, single prompt, 6 needles — a real but small retrieval probe, not a naive-vs-adaptive strategy comparison (that lives inside the model's own context mgmt).

Modern models often retrieve well; high recall here validates that the rot PREMISE is not catastrophic for this model/length, it does not by itself prove the adaptive layer's advantage — that remains the deterministic benchmark's claim.
Token-level utilization is not precisely controlled; the filler length is fixed.
live_measurement.source: real mimo needle-in-haystack retrieval over a long filler context

external_baseline.reason: No authorized external Fable 5 baseline run is available in this repo.

basis: research proposition 1: adaptive context management vs context rot
at 70% utilization, naive accuracy 57% (documented ~40% dumb zone)
adaptive (lossy compression + relevance filtering) 85% expected accuracy with a seed band
red-teamed: compression loss is modeled, not assumed away; deterministic retention benchmark, not a live-LLM run
